title: A photograph from a grandmother's wedding
summary: Reflect on an old family photograph and what it makes you think about.
topic: An old black-and-white photograph from a family wedding
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences reflecting on an old family photograph. Describe what you see in the photo, say what you know about the people in it, and explain how the photo makes you feel. Use past simple and simple present.
hintStarters: The photograph is black and white, and the people in it are…
My grandmother told me that the picture was taken…
When I look at this photo, I think about…
sampleText: The photograph is black and white and sits in a small cardboard frame on my father's bookshelf. It shows a wedding in a village in Moravia in 1952. My grandfather stands on the left in a dark suit that is slightly too big for him. My grandmother is beside him in a white dress and a veil. She looks very young. Neither of them is smiling. I always thought this was strange until my father explained that people did not usually smile in photographs back then. It was considered a serious occasion. My grandfather died before I was born, so I only know him from pictures like this one. I recognise my father's eyes in his face. On the back of the photograph, someone wrote the date and the names of the guests in pencil, but the handwriting is difficult to read now. There are about thirty people in the image, standing in two rows in front of a stone church. Some of the women wear headscarves. Several of the men hold their hats in their hands. I do not know any of the other faces. But I look at this photo sometimes and feel a strange connection — all these people gathered on one day, and I exist partly because of that day.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Mixing present simple and past simple
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong bài này, present simple mô tả những gì người viết nhìn thấy trong ảnh bây giờ ('The photograph sits', 'My grandfather stands'), trong khi past simple mô tả những sự kiện đã xảy ra trong quá khứ ('died', 'explained', 'wrote'). Việc kết hợp hai thì này là rất tự nhiên khi bạn vừa nhìn vào một vật cụ thể vừa nhớ về lịch sử.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: In this sample, present simple describes what the writer sees in the photo now ('The photograph sits', 'My grandfather stands'), while past simple describes past events ('died', 'explained', 'wrote'). Mixing these two tenses is natural when you look at a physical object and reflect on its history.

sampleTranslationVi: Tấm ảnh đen trắng nằm trong một khung bìa cứng nhỏ trên kệ sách của cha tôi. Nó chụp một đám cưới ở một ngôi làng tại Moravia năm 1952. Ông nội tôi đứng bên trái trong một bộ com lê tối màu hơi rộng so với ông. Bà nội đứng cạnh ông trong chiếc váy trắng và mạng che mặt. Bà trông rất trẻ. Không ai trong số họ mỉm cười. Tôi luôn thấy điều này lạ cho đến khi cha tôi giải thích rằng mọi người thường không mỉm cười trong ảnh thời đó. Đó được coi là một dịp trang trọng. Ông nội tôi mất trước khi tôi được sinh ra, nên tôi chỉ biết ông qua những tấm ảnh như tấm này. Tôi nhận ra đôi mắt của cha tôi trong khuôn mặt ông. Ở mặt sau tấm ảnh, ai đó đã viết ngày tháng và tên các vị khách bằng bút chì, nhưng chữ viết tay bây giờ khó đọc. Có khoảng ba mươi người trong ảnh, đứng thành hai hàng trước một nhà thờ đá. Một số phụ nữ đội khăn quàng đầu. Một số người đàn ông cầm mũ trên tay. Tôi không biết bất kỳ khuôn mặt nào khác. Nhưng tôi nhìn vào tấm ảnh này đôi khi và cảm thấy một mối liên hệ kỳ lạ — tất cả những người này tụ họp vào một ngày, và tôi tồn tại một phần vì ngày hôm đó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best sentence to introduce a photograph you are looking at.
options: The photograph shows a wedding in a village in 1952.
The photograph showed a wedding in a village in 1952.
The photograph is showing a wedding in a village in 1952.
The photograph has shown a wedding in a village in 1952.
mcQuestions.explanation: When describing what a photograph contains (a permanent fact), use present simple: 'shows'. Past or perfect tenses imply the photo no longer exists or the action is finished.
mcQuestions.hint: Is the content of a photograph a permanent fact or a past event?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'neither of them'?
options: Neither of them is smiling.
Neither of them are smiling.
Neither of they is smiling.
Neither of them smiles.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Neither of them' is followed by a singular verb ('is') in formal/standard English. 'They' is wrong after 'of'; it must be the object pronoun 'them'.
mcQuestions.hint: Is 'neither of' followed by a singular or plural verb in standard English?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the correct sentence about someone who died before the speaker was born.
options: He died before I was born.
He died before I born.
He died before I am born.
He died before I have been born.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Before I was born' uses past simple in the subordinate clause. 'Born' alone or 'am born' or 'have been born' are all wrong in this context.
mcQuestions.hint: What tense goes in the 'before' clause when referring to a past time?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that correctly describes recognising a feature in a face.
options: I recognise my father's eyes in his face.
I recognise my father's eyes on his face.
I recognise my father's eyes at his face.
I recognise my father's eyes from his face.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Recognise something in something' is the natural phrase for finding a feature within a broader context.
mcQuestions.hint: Which preposition means you found a feature within a larger thing (a face)?

options: The handwriting is difficult to read.
The handwriting is difficult for read.
The handwriting is difficult reading.
The handwriting is difficult that we read.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Difficult to + base form' is the standard structure: 'difficult to read', 'easy to understand'. 'For read' and 'difficult reading' are both wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: What structure follows 'difficult' when describing how hard an action is?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that uses 'several of' correctly.
options: Several of the men hold their hats in their hands.
Several of the men holds their hats in their hands.
Several of men hold their hats in their hands.
Severals of the men hold their hats in their hands.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Several of the men' is the correct form — 'several of' must be followed by a definite noun phrase ('the men'). 'Severals' is not a word; 'holds' is wrong for a plural subject.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'several of', does the noun need a determiner like 'the'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word describes someone whose face shows no warmth or emotion?
options: proud
stern
unfamiliar
worn
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stern' describes a serious, cold, or unsmiling expression. 'Proud' means showing self-respect; 'unfamiliar' means unknown; 'worn' means damaged from use.
mcQuestions.hint: Which adjective describes a serious, unsmiling face?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence that correctly explains why the writer exists.
options: I exist partly because of that day.
I exist partly because that day.
I exist partly because of this day.
I exist partly thanks for that day.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Because of' is followed by a noun phrase. 'Because' alone needs a full clause. 'Thanks for' is not used in this structure; 'thanks to' would be an alternative.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'because of', do you use a noun or a full clause?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence about people standing in a group.
options: They are standing in two rows in front of a church.
They are standing in two lines in front of a church.
They are standing in two rows in the front of a church.
They are standing in two rows before the front of a church.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In front of' means facing or before something. 'In the front of' usually means inside the front section of something. 'Rows' is the standard word for lines of people in a photograph.
mcQuestions.hint: Does 'in front of' need a definite article 'the' after 'in'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means 'something that shows a connection to the past'?
options: evidence
caption
frame
occasion
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Evidence' is something that shows proof or connection to something else. A 'caption' is text under a photo; a 'frame' holds the photo; an 'occasion' is a special event.
mcQuestions.hint: Which word means 'proof or sign that something existed'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that correctly says something was not done in the past.
options: People did not usually smile in photographs back then.
People did not use to smile in photographs back then.
People not usually smiled in photographs back then.
People did not usually smiled in photographs back then.
mcQuestions.explanation: Simple negative past: 'did not + base form'. 'Did not usually smile' is correct. 'Did not usually smiled' uses the wrong form after 'did not'. Option B is grammatically possible but changes the meaning slightly (used to).
mcQuestions.hint: After 'did not', which form of the verb is correct?

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer says, 'I exist partly because of that day.' Looking at a very old family photo, do you ever feel connected to people you never met? Why might old photographs create this feeling?
